fr:mainmatter.html:p: A function f\colon A \to B is called n-truncated when all of its fibers are n-truncated types. By analogy, we shall also refer to maps as contractible when their fibers are; we shall refer to maps as propositional when their fibers are propositions.

A type A is said to be contractible with centre a:A when every x:A is equipped with an identification \chi _x\colon a = x. We say that A is contractible when it comes equipped with some a:A such that A is contractible with centre a. We sometimes refer to a contractible type as a singleton.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
A type A is called a set when for each x,y:A the identification type x=y is a proposition.
The notions of contractible and propositional types generate an infinite hierarchy of truncation levels expressing the point after which higher identifications become trivial.
A truncation level is an integer n \geq -2.
Given a truncation level n, we define what it means for a type to be n-truncated by induction:

html:ul.html:li: a type is -2-truncated when it is contractible;
a type is -1-truncated when it is a proposition;
for n \geq 0, a type A is n-truncated when for every x,y:A the identification type x=y is n-1-truncated.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: So, in particular, a 0-truncated type is precisely a set. We shall often refer to n-truncated types as n-types.

A function f\colon A \to B is called n-truncated when all of its fibers are n-truncated types. By analogy, we shall also refer to maps as contractible when their fibers are; we shall refer to maps as propositional when their fibers are propositions.

fr:title.text: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: By Lemma , we may argue that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of a proposition. We let P be the following type defined using path object structure of fibers given in Example :
It is not difficult to show using Definition that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of P; we will not need the further fact that it is equivalent to P.
Any embedding f\colon A\to B is propositional.
Let \alpha \colon fa_0 = fa_1 be the concatenation p_0\cdot p_1^{-1}. Therefore we may define:

To define \psi \colon p_0=\mathsf {act}_{f}\phi \cdot p_1 we use the counit of our inverse as we have \epsilon \alpha \colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\phi = p_0\cdot p_1^{-1}. Thus concatenating on either side with p_1 we have our goal, modulo the laws of identification concatenation and symmetry respectively.
Any propositional map f\colon A\to B is an embedding.

Fixing x,y:A we must show that \mathsf {act}_{f}\colon x = y \to fx=fy is invertible. By Lemma and Lemma , it suffices to exhibit a biinverse to \mathsf {act}_{f} (it is convenient that the underlying maps of the section and retraction need not be identical).
A function f\colon A\to B is called bijective when it is a surjective propositional map.
Any bijection f\colon A\to B is contractible.

Fixing b:B, we must check that the fiber of f over b is contractible. For the centre of contraction, we must construct an element of this fiber; because f is propositional by Lemma , the fiber is a proposition, so it suffices by the induction principle for propositional truncation to observe that the fiber is inhabited because f is surjective.
For any type A, the identity map 1_{A}\colon A\to A is contractible.
Immediate by identification induction.

fr:title.text: The identity map is n-truncated for all n

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The identity map is n-truncated for all n

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For any type A, the identity map 1_{A}\colon A\to A is n-truncated for all n.

We proceed by induction on the truncation level n: when n=-2, we recall that the identity map is contractible, and in the inductive step we apply the upward closure of n-truncation to each fiber of 1_{A}.
Any biinvertible map f\colon A\to B is contractible.
As biinvertible maps are quasi-invertible and quasi-invertible maps are contractible.
Any biinvertible map f\colon A \to B is propositional.
We must show that each fiber of f is a proposition; in fact, each fiber is contractible as biinvertible maps are contractible, and contractible types are propositions.
Any bijection f\colon A\to B is biinvertible.
By Lemma , bijections are contractible; by Corollary , bijections are contractible.
Any quasi-invertible map f\colon A\to B is contractible.
Any quasi-inverse can be improved to a coherent inverse, and any coherently invertible map is contractible.
Let f\colon A\to B be a section of some r\colon B\to A; the type of sections of f is, then, a proposition.

Using the univalence principle for dependent function spaces, we can see that the type of sections of f\colon A\to B is a retract of the fiber of f\circ - at 1_{B}, using the univalence principle for sections.

To show that the type of sections of f is a proposition, we may assume beforehand that we have such a section of f; therefore, because we have assumed a retraction of f already, we know that f is biinvertible and therefore that f is contractible. By Lemma , it suffices to show that the fiber of f\circ - at 1_{B} is a proposition, which follows via Lemma and Lemma .
Any contractible map f\colon A \to B is a biinvertible.
Because contractible maps are both sections and retractions.
Any contractible map f\colon A \to B is a retraction.

Let f\colon A\to B be contractible; we define a section s\colon B\to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. The witness h_s\colon f\circ s\sim 1_{B} exhibiting s as a section of f, we use the second component of the centre of contraction.
Any contractible map f\colon A \to B is a section.

Let f\colon A\to B be contractible; we define a retraction r\colon B\to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. To construct h_r\colon r\circ f\sim 1_{A} exhibiting r as a retraction of f, we use the identification between any two elements of the fiber of f over fx.
Any invertible map f\colon A\to B is contractible.

fr:title.text: Being an n-truncated map is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being an n-truncated map is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: That a given map f\colon A \to B is n-truncated is a proposition.

The type of isomorphisms onto C is inhabited, considering the identity isomorphism 1_{C}\colon C\cong C. Therefore, it suffices to show that the type of isomorphisms onto C depicted below is a proposition:
We unfold the definitions of a precategories and their isomorphisms, dropping propositional axioms:
A function that is both propositional and a surjection, i.e. a bijection, is an equivalence by Corollary . We can therefore repackage our telescope equivalently:
Next we rewrite using the univalence principles for functions and families of types (Axiom , Axiom ):
By the univalence principle for functions, the data above is contractible.
The fiber of a function f\colon A \to B at an element b:B is the type consisting of elements a\colon A equipped with identifications p\colon fa=b.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
Given a truncation level n, we define what it means for a type to be n-truncated by induction:

html:ul.html:li: a type is -2-truncated when it is contractible;
a type is -1-truncated when it is a proposition;
for n \geq 0, a type A is n-truncated when for every x,y:A the identification type x=y is n-1-truncated.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: So, in particular, a 0-truncated type is precisely a set. We shall often refer to n-truncated types as n-types.
